title: Incremental MJ workflow beats single mega-prompts — isolate variables, build in layers
body:
I discovered through hands-on Midjourney work that incremental building is the way to go. Instead of one mega-prompt where I cram scene, character, props, and rendering into a single text string and hope for the best, I now build images in 3-4 layered runs. The text foundation sets the parametric envelope — aspect ratio, lighting, mood, camera, DOF. Then a bare stage photo sets the scene. Then the sref adds Pixar rendering. Then the omni ref brings in the character. Each layer is a decision point where I can see exactly what that input contributed.

The key insight: text is good at parameters (dimensions, color temperature, camera angle). Images are good at spatial and atmospheric information. An actual photo of a bare golden room communicates more than any text description. Mixing them in text produces unreliable results.

This came from noticing that when I dropped a NYC skyline photo as an image prompt, MJ instantly nailed perspective and background. Text never got close to that reliability. So the workflow flipped: text sets rules, images set content.

The results speak for themselves — I produced 4 characters (Artist, Connector, Engineer, Explorer) in one session using the new approach, compared to 3 characters in a much longer session using the old single-prompt method. And when something was wrong, I knew exactly which layer to fix.
decision: Build MJ images in 3-4 layered runs (stage → rendering → character → props) instead of single mega-prompts.
alternatives_considered: Single mega-prompt approach from V6 doc.
open_questions: Does the layered approach work equally well at 1:2 portrait and 2:1 wide ratios?
